title: Fraktur minuscule, the German blackletter hand
subtitle: The small-letter form of the German broken script
excerpt: Fraktur is the predominant German blackletter style from the 16th century onward. The minuscule letterforms are constructed from a specific set of hairline entry strokes and broken-diamond finials, quite different from the Northern Gothic textura quadrata covered elsewhere.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), background on historical German hands. Rudolf Koch's lettering guides, early 20th century, contain some of the most precise analysis of Fraktur letterforms; Koch's work predates 1928 and portions were reproduced in German craft publications now in the public domain. This tutorial draws on the structural analysis consistent with those pre-1928 sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A German blackletter style characterised by pointed, angular forms with distinctive hairline entry strokes and broken diamond-shaped finials.
An extremely thin stroke made by the corner of a broad-edge nib. In Fraktur the hairline entry is a defining structural feature.
The terminal decorative element at the end of a stroke. Fraktur uses a broken-diamond finial formed by lifting and re-placing the nib.

content.text: Fraktur dominated German printing from around 1520 to the mid-20th century. Its distinctive appearance comes from two features: the hairline entry stroke that precedes every main body stroke, and the broken-diamond
at the base of the downstrokes. Both features require precise nib handling that differs substantially from the smoother movements of Foundational or Italic.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (medium)
items.notes: A medium broad-edge nib. Fraktur's compressed letterforms work well at x-heights of 5 nib-widths.

items.name: Straight pen holder
items.notes: Fraktur is written with a straight holder at approximately 45° pen angle to the baseline.

items.name: Sumi ink
items.notes: Dense black for the highest contrast. Iron-gall ink also suits Fraktur's historical associations.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.notes: Ruled at 5 nib-widths for the x-height, with ascender and descender zones each 3 nib-widths.

content.text: Hold the broad-edge nib at 45° from the baseline throughout. Unlike Foundational (which varies to ~30° for horizontal strokes) or Italic (which uses ~45°),
holds 45° for every stroke, the thick-to-thin contrast is generated purely by the nib's natural response at that angle.
Every Fraktur downstroke begins with a

entry. Place only the corner of the nib on the paper at the ascender line (or x-height line for lowercase). Draw a short diagonal upstroke to the right, just 1.5-2 nib-widths, before swinging the full nib face downward into the body stroke. The hairline is thin because only the corner point carries ink; it transitions to full stroke width as the nib rotates.

At the base of each downstroke, press the nib gently into the paper, lift slightly, then press again at 45° to the first position. This produces a small diamond-shaped terminus, the characteristic Fraktur foot. The two-step action (press, lift, press) is what makes the diamond 'broken'. Practise this on its own before attempting letterforms, it reads as a single gesture once internalised.

Fraktur's minuscule is built from four strokes: the full downstroke (i-body), the arched upstroke that follows the downstroke (n-arch), the curved downstroke with a retracted foot (c and e shape), and the loop ascender or descender. Every letter is an assembly of these four movements. Begin with the i, hairline entry, full downstroke, broken-diamond finial, then build n, m, and u before attempting the rounded forms c, e, and o.

items.symptom: The hairline entry bleeds into a thick mark
items.cause: Too much ink on the nib, or the nib angle dropped below 45° on the approach stroke.
items.fix: Hold 45° precisely for the approach. Blot the nib on scrap paper before the entry stroke if ink is pooling.

items.symptom: The broken-diamond finial looks like a blob
items.cause: The two-step press-lift-press is too fast or the second press is too light.
items.fix: Slow down the finial, the pause between the two presses is where the diamond forms.

items.symptom: Letters look Gothic textura rather than Fraktur
items.cause: Lacking the hairline entries, the letters are starting flat rather than with a diagonal approach.
items.fix: Isolate the approach stroke. Drill it separately on ruled paper until it becomes automatic before combining with the body stroke.
